question: A long-running server forks child processes to handle concurrent tasks. Over time, ps shows many processes in Z state, and eventually fork fails with EAGAIN. What is a zombie process, why does the kernel create them instead of immediately freeing terminated process entries, and how can the parent prevent this accumulation?
answer: Zombie processes are terminated children whose exit status was not collected by the parent via wait or waitpid. The kernel preserves the PID, exit code, and resource usage for collection. Zombies use no CPU or memory but consume a PID slot. PID exhaustion causes fork to return EAGAIN. Identify with ps aux filtered for Z. Fix with a SIGCHLD handler that loops on waitpid with WNOHANG. Calling wait or a single waitpid misses children because SIGCHLD coalesces multiple exits into one signal.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  P[Parent forks child process] --> C[Child runs and terminates]
  C --> Z[Kernel marks child as zombie state Z]
  Z --> W{Parent calls waitpid?}
  W -->|Yes: collects exit status| F[Process table entry freed]
  W -->|No: parent does not reap| A[Zombie persists indefinitely]
  A -->|PID table fills up| E[fork returns EAGAIN]
  A -->|gdb emergency reap| G[gdb -p parent_pid\ncall waitpid-1 0 WNOHANG]
  G --> F
  A -->|SIGCHLD handler| H[sigchld handler loop\nwhile waitpid > 0]
  H --> F
